Map Rotation

Your Name

2026-03-14

Source: vignettes/rotation.Rmd
rotation.Rmd
library(ggbasemap)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
#> Linking to GEOS 3.13.1, GDAL 3.11.4, PROJ 9.7.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE

# Load sample data
nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf"), quiet = TRUE)

Introduction

Map rotation allows you to present your spatial data at an angle, which can be useful for:

  • Fitting rectangular maps better on page layouts
  • Emphasizing certain geographic features
  • Creating visually interesting presentations
  • Aligning with natural geographic orientations (e.g., coastlines, mountain ranges)

ggbasemap uses the Oblique Mercator projection to achieve smooth rotation while maintaining accurate geographic relationships.

Basic Rotation

Simple Example

To rotate a map, use the coord_rotate() function:

# Get rotation parameters for 45 degrees
rot <- coord_rotate(nc, angle = 45)

# Create the rotated map
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot$coord +
  ggtitle("North Carolina rotated 45°")

What coord_rotate() Returns

The coord_rotate() function returns a list with:

  • coord: A coord_sf object that applies the rotation
  • bbox: An expanded bounding box that covers the rotated area
  • crs: The coordinate reference system string used
  • angle: The rotation angle in degrees

Understanding the Components

The Expanded Bounding Box

When you rotate a map, the corners extend beyond the original bounding box. The bbox returned by coord_rotate() is expanded to ensure the entire rotated map is covered:

# Original bbox
orig_bbox <- sf::st_bbox(nc)
print(orig_bbox)

# Rotated bbox (notice it's larger)
rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)
print(rot$bbox)

This expanded bbox is used by add_basemap() to fetch enough tiles to cover the entire rotated view.

The Coordinate System

The rotation uses the Oblique Mercator projection with these parameters:

  • lat_0: Latitude of the centroid
  • lonc: Longitude of the centroid
  • gamma: Rotation angle in degrees
  • alpha: Zero (no skew)
  • k: Scale factor (1.0)

This projection preserves: - ✅ Local shapes and angles - ✅ Straight lines (great circles) - ✅ Scale along the central line

Rotation Angles

Common Angles 

# No rotation (0°)
rot0 <- coord_rotate(nc, 0)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot0$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot0$coord +
  ggtitle("0° (No Rotation)")

# 30°
rot30 <- coord_rotate(nc, 30)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot30$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot30$coord +
  ggtitle("30° Rotation")

# 45°
rot45 <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot45$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot45$coord +
  ggtitle("45° Rotation")

# 60°
rot60 <- coord_rotate(nc, 60)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot60$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot60$coord +
  ggtitle("60° Rotation")

# 90°
rot90 <- coord_rotate(nc, 90)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot90$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot90$coord +
  ggtitle("90° Rotation")

Negative Angles

You can also use negative angles for clockwise rotation:

# -45° (clockwise)
rot_neg45 <- coord_rotate(nc, -45)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot_neg45$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot_neg45$coord +
  ggtitle("-45° (Clockwise)")

Angles > 360

Angles greater than 360 are automatically normalized:

# 405° = 45°
rot405 <- coord_rotate(nc, 405)  # Same as 45°

Practical Examples

Example 1: Fitting a Page Layout

Sometimes your data is oriented at an angle that doesn’t fit well in a standard rectangular plot:

# Create elongated data
elongated_data <- nc

# Original (wastes space)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(elongated_data) +
  geom_sf(data = elongated_data) +
  ggtitle("Original (wasted space on sides)")

# Rotated (better fit)
rot <- coord_rotate(elongated_data, 30)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(elongated_data, bbox = rot$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = elongated_data) +
  rot$coord +
  ggtitle("Rotated 30° (better fit)")

Example 2: Aligning with Coastlines

For coastal areas, rotating to align with the coastline can be visually effective:

# Hypothetical coastal data
rot <- coord_rotate(coastal_data, -15)  # Align with coastline
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(coastal_data, bbox = rot$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = coastal_data) +
  rot$coord +
  ggtitle("Aligned with Coastline")

Example 3: Emphasizing a Road or River

Rotate to align with linear features:

# Align with a major highway
rot <- coord_rotate(region_data, 25)
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(region_data, bbox = rot$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = major_road, color = "red", size = 2) +
  geom_sf(data = region_data, fill = NA) +
  rot$coord

Advanced Rotation

Combining with Transformations

Rotation works perfectly with image transformations:

rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)

ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, 
              bbox = rot$bbox,
              grayscale = TRUE,
              contrast = 1.2) +
  geom_sf(data = nc, fill = NA, color = "red") +
  rot$coord +
  ggtitle("Rotated + Styled")

Multiple Layers

You can add multiple layers on top of the rotated basemap:

rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 30)

ggplot() +
  # Base: rotated basemap
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox, alpha = 0.7) +
  # Layer 1: county boundaries
  geom_sf(data = nc, aes(fill = area), alpha = 0.5) +
  # Layer 2: major roads
  geom_sf(data = roads, color = "red", size = 1) +
  # Layer 3: cities
  geom_sf(data = cities, aes(size = population), color = "blue") +
  # Apply rotation to all
  rot$coord +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  theme_void()

Custom Styling 

rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)

ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, 
              bbox = rot$bbox,
              zoom = 12,
              saturation = 0.5) +
  geom_sf(data = nc, 
          fill = NA, 
          color = "white",
          linewidth = 1) +
  rot$coord +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  ) +
  ggtitle("Night Mode Style",
          subtitle = "45° rotation") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(color = "white", size = 20),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray70", size = 14)
  )

The Rotation Matrix

The package also provides a rot() function that creates a 2D rotation matrix:

# Create rotation matrix for 45 degrees
m <- rot(45)
print(m)

# Apply to points
points <- matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 1), ncol = 2)
rotated_points <- points %*% m

This is used internally but can be useful for custom calculations.

Performance Considerations

Tile Fetching

Rotated maps require more tiles because the bounding box is expanded:

  • 0° rotation: Minimum tiles
  • 45° rotation: ~1.4x more tiles (diagonal expansion)
  • Any rotation: More tiles than no rotation

This means: - Rotated maps load slightly slower - Use reasonable zoom levels - Cache results when possible

Memory Usage

The expanded bounding box means: - Larger raster objects - More memory usage - Slower rendering

For large areas or high zoom levels, consider: - Using lower zoom (11-13 instead of 15+) - Cropping data before rotation - Using padding = 0.05 instead of default 0.1

Troubleshooting

Issue: Map Looks Cropped

Problem: Parts of your data are cut off

Solution: The expanded bbox should prevent this, but if not:

# Increase padding manually
rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 45)
# Expand bbox further
rot$bbox <- rot$bbox * 1.1  # Add 10% more

ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot$coord

Issue: Tiles Not Loading

Problem: Rotation works but tiles don’t appear

Solution: Check that you’re using the bbox from coord_rotate():

# Wrong - uses original bbox
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc) +  # Missing bbox!
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot$coord

# Correct - uses expanded bbox
ggplot() +
  add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +  # Correct!
  geom_sf(data = nc) +
  rot$coord

Issue: Text Labels Rotate Too

Problem: Text labels on your map rotate with the map

Solution: This is expected! Everything rotates together. If you need horizontal text, add it after applying the coordinate system with annotate() or use geom_text() with hjust and vjust adjustments.

Best Practices

1. Choose the Right Angle

  • 0-15°: Subtle, good for minor adjustments
  • 15-45°: Moderate, good for fitting page layouts
  • 45-60°: Strong rotation, dramatic effect
  • 60-90°: Very strong, use sparingly

2. Test Multiple Angles 

# Try several angles to find the best
angles <- c(0, 15, 30, 45)
plots <- lapply(angles, function(a) {
  rot <- coord_rotate(nc, a)
  ggplot() +
    add_basemap(nc, bbox = rot$bbox) +
    geom_sf(data = nc) +
    rot$coord +
    ggtitle(paste(a, "degrees"))
})

# View all with patchwork or gridExtra

3. Consider Your Audience

  • Academic papers: Subtle rotation (0-20°)
  • Presentations: Moderate rotation (20-45°)
  • Marketing/Design: Any angle that works

4. Combine with Other Features

Rotation works great with: - Image transformations (grayscale, contrast) - Different tile sources - Multiple data layers - Custom themes

Complete Example

Here’s a complete, publication-ready example:

library(ggbasemap)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
library(viridis)

# Load data
nc <- st_read(system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf"))

# Calculate rotation (30 degrees looks good for this shape)
rot <- coord_rotate(nc, 30)

# Create the map
map <- ggplot() +
  # Base layer: styled basemap
  add_basemap(nc, 
              bbox = rot$bbox,
              zoom = 9,
              saturation = 0.4,
              brightness = 1.05,
              contrast = 1.1) +
  # Data layer: counties with fill
  geom_sf(data = nc, 
          aes(fill = AREA),
          color = "white",
          linewidth = 0.3,
          alpha = 0.8) +
  # Apply rotation
  rot$coord +
  # Color scale
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma",
                       name = "Area (sq km)",
                       labels = scales::comma) +
  # Theme
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0.5, color = "gray40")
  ) +
  # Labels
  labs(title = "North Carolina Counties",
       subtitle = "Rotated 30° for optimal layout",
       caption = "Data: North Carolina county boundaries")

# Save
#ggsave("nc_rotated_map.png", map, width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 300)

print(map)

Next Steps

  • Read about Image Transformations to style your basemaps
  • Check Advanced Usage for power user features
  • See FAQ for common questions

References

  • Snyder, J. P. (1987). Map Projections: A Working Manual
  • OpenStreetMap Wiki: Mercator Projection
  • PROJ documentation: Oblique Mercator
